content: img src    class  ieee-summary   h2 Summary  h2  ul  li Joby Aviation is realizing Uber s original  Elevate  dream, moving electric vertical take-off and landing  eVTOL  aircraft from science fiction toward commercial reality.  li  li By 2026,  a href   joby  Joby aims to inaugurate the world s first integrated air taxi network  a  in Dubai leveraging aggressive local infrastructure investment to bypass Western bureaucratic hurdles.  li  li  a href   agreement  The plan includes  vertiports  at strategic hubs like Dubai International Airport  a , creating the essential physical and digital ecosystem required for reliable point-to-point urban flight.  li  li  a href   groundwork  While facing a cautious FAA in the U.S.  a , Joby will use its Dubai operations to bridge the gap between experimental testing and full-scale passenger operations.  li   ul   div  p class  drop-caps   strong Ten years ago,  strong  ride-sharing giant a href    target  _blank   Uber  a  embraced a sci-fi future in which clean, quiet electric aircraft would shuttle passengers around crowded cities. Uber s well-funded Elevate initiative, which included a href    target  _blank   a white paper  a  and three high-profile annual summits, effectively launched the electric vertical take-off and landing a href    target  _self    eVTOL  industry  a , promising investors, regulators, and the general public that these futuristic flying taxis were   a href    target  _blank  closer than you think  a .   p  div class  rm-embed embed-media   iframe height  110px  id  noa-web-audio-player  src    style  border  none  width  100     iframe   div  p  span At the time, California-based  span  a href    target  _blank   Joby Aviation  a  span  was still in stealth mode. But behind the scenes, this pioneering eVTOL developer which has received more than US  3 billion in total funding, including  span  a href    target  _blank   around  900 million from Toyota  a  span  was playing a major role in shaping Uber s vision. It later stepped in to keep that vision alive,  span  a href    target  _blank   acquiring the Elevate program  a  span  in 2020 after Uber CEO  span  a href    target  _blank   Dara Khosrowshahi  a  span  decided to axe it.  span   p  p class  rm-anchors  id  joby  Now, Joby, which was founded in 2009 and has become the dominant eVTOL startup, says it is finally on the verge of making  urban air mobility  a reality. It plans to conduct its first passenger flights in 2026 in Dubai, United Arab Emirates.  p  div class  ieee-sidebar-small   p This article is part of our special report  a href    target  _blank  Top Tech 2026  a .  p   div  p  Dubai continues to be our global launchpad for commercial service, and our progress here is a testament to the UAE s visionary approach to advanced air mobility,  says a href    target  _blank   Anthony Khoury  a , Joby s UAE general manager, in an email interview.  Dubai is on track to be the first city in the world to offer a fully integrated, premium air taxi network, and we are sprinting toward that target.   p  h2 Joby Struck a Six-Year Exclusive Deal with Dubai  h2  p The company first  a href    rel  noopener noreferrer  target  _blank  announced its UAE plans  a  at the World Governments Summit in Dubai in February 2024, striking a deal with Dubai s Roads and Transport Authority  RTA  that gives it an exclusive right to operate air taxis there for six years from the launch of commercial operations.  p  p class  pull-quote   span Joby s exclusive Dubai deal will help fortify its lead in the global race to commercialize electric air taxis   span   p  p class  rm-anchors  id  agreement  Joby also signed an agreement with U.K.-based a href    target  _blank   Skyports  a  to design, build, and operate four   a href    target  _blank  vertiport  a   sites in Dubai places for the eVTOL aircraft to load and unload passengers and charge their batteries. The first vertiport will be near Dubai International Airport, with additional ones planned for Dubai Mall, the Atlantis the Royal resort, and American University in Dubai.  p  p Joby won t be the first eVTOL developer to carry passengers. That distinction goes to China s EHang, which is already conducting limited sightseeing and a href    target  _blank   demonstration flights  a  with its two-seat, autonomous electric multicopters.  Joby s aircraft are piloted.  If Joby pulls off its goal, however, it will be the first to routinely fly passengers from point to point over urban traffic, in keeping with Uber Elevate s original vision. Its exclusive agreement in Dubai will help fortify its lead in the global race to commercialize electric air taxis, which includes a handful of other Western eVTOL developers, plus a growing number of Chinese players. Besides its Dubai deal, Joby also has a partnership with Delta to start an airport shuttle service in the United States.  p  p class  shortcode-media shortcode-media-rebelmouse-image    img alt  A futuristic-looking electric rotorcraft is seen flying in a blue sky.  class  rm-shortcode  data-rm-shortcode-id  392311d6e558ebbe4bd6e828f1f0a3eb  data-rm-shortcode-name  rebelmouse-image  id  b31d3  loading  lazy  src    class  image-media media-caption  placeholder  Add Photo Caption...  The Joby S4 electric vertical takeoff and landing  eVTOL  aircraft has six electric motors, each weighing 28 kilograms and capable of a peak output of 236 kilowatts.  small  small class  image-media media-photo-credit  placeholder  Add Photo Credit...  Joby Aviation  small   p  p Operating a reliable air taxi service is a demanding proposition that will require Joby s aircraft, charging infrastructure, and scheduling software to perform safely and reliably day in and day out. Since every new and complex technology has teething problems, Joby envisions fairly limited initial operations in 2026.  p  p  We will transition from test flights to more complex proving runs and eventually nonpaying passenger flights out of the completed vertiports, ensuring a seamless passenger experience ahead of full commercial launch,  says Khoury. He adds that Joby is currently working with Skyports to ready its initial vertiports and with government agencies in Dubai and the UAE to receive the necessary approvals for its operations.  p  p  Dubai s approach is deeper and more comprehensive than what you see in many of the headlines,  said Clint Harper, an aviation infrastructure and policy advisor who recently participated in an advanced air mobility workshop with Dubai s RTA.  In our workshop,  he says,  the RTA staff had fantastic questions and concerns regarding safety, security, and system-level integration. Everyone recognized and appreciated strong government support and wanted to deliver the right system solution, not just a one-off demo. I was thoroughly impressed and inspired.   p  h2 Initial Air Operations Will Precede an Airworthiness Certificate  h2  p class  rm-anchors  id  groundwork  Notably, all of this groundwork is taking place in advance of Joby receiving an initial type certificate for its aircraft from the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration. In the United States  and elsewhere , a type certificate is typically a prerequisite for conducting commercial operations with paying passengers. Joby claims it s making good progress toward FAA certification, but how quickly  or slowly  that process moves is largely out of its hands. In recent years, the FAA has been taking longer to certify even conventional airplanes and helicopters, which the industry blames on staffing shortages at the agency and more cautious decision-making in the wake of the a href    target  _blank   Boeing 737 Max crisis  a .  p  p This perception that certification delays have more to do with bureaucracy than safety may be why Dubai is willing to approve some early operations by Joby in advance of FAA type certification. Interestingly, the United States is now following the UAE s example. In September, the FAA and U.S. Department of Transportation began soliciting proposals for an a href    target  _blank   eVTOL Integration Pilot Program  a   eIPP , which will select at least five projects to demonstrate eVTOL operations in the national airspace starting as early as summer 2026.  p  p The FAA has a href    target  _blank   stated  a  that the eIPP won t allow eVTOL developers to bypass certification requirements or carry paying passengers. However, it will enable them to undertake additional testing and demonstration flights as a stepping-stone to commercial operations. Joby says it s a href    target  _blank   planning to take part  a  in the eIPP, meaning its air taxis could also be flying over U.S. cities in 2026 even if the only person on board is the pilot.  p

metadata.author: Elan Head
